item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy smokes, this is awesome. This is some of the best feedback I've ever gotten; it's so specific, so laser-focused.

There's definitely a 4410 revision on the horizon after seeing this, and I think that it'll encourage me to be more conscientious in the future, think twice before rattling off a really complicated sentence.

Thanks so much for the time you put in to this comment!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m a little annoyed that the comments are mostly just about pataphysics, and not giving feedback on broader issues. Some people have mentioned length, which is an issue, but the major culprit in that area is word bloat: Text that serves no purpose in terms of the narrative.

Every single word should serve a purpose – advancing the narrative or providing necessary new information or context, or else it’s a waste of space or a waste and the reader’s time.

For examples, I’m going to do a line-by-line of everything up to the first tab with my comments in blue.

+ show block
– hide block


NOTICE FROM THE RECORDS AND INFORMATION SECURITY ADMINISTRATION
You are attempting to access a Foundation Special Database and network. Access to this database requires special departmental clearance. All access attempts are recorded with RAISA. Please enter your credentials.
user: Z. Saxon, Level 3, id: 9722410 Rho
pass: 1twothr3e4ourtheta'walls_brainbugs_jack0ftales_starw1zards_fullarc_denouement@thefinalfrontier
Credentials Valid…
User possesses LAMPSHADE Class clearance…
Department of Pataphysics membership confirmed. Welcome Dr. Saxon.
.call Enkidu
Hello. I am Enkidu.aic, an Artificially Intelligent Construct used by the Foundation Department of Pataphysics for narrativistic analysis and data archiving. How can I help you today?
Hey Enkidu, are you busy?

Not particularly. Looking through what the webcrawlers are giving us, searching for tropes, character arcs, and plot devices. The usual. What do you need Zack?
Oh it's real simple, don't want to distract you from your work too much. I just need the major iterations of SCP-4410's abbreviated file, maintaining the portions relating to its artistic development and history.

In “too much”, we’re seeing wasted space in terms of adverbs that are already implied by the verb.

Alright, but first I've got to ask. Does the black moon howl?
When it need not convey a meaning, only inspire unity.
Sorry about that, I have to do it to everyone. Got to make sure you're clean and all.
It's totally fine. Whatever you feel like you need to do, I trust that it needs to be done.

This interaction is cute, which is good, but we’re veering into self-commentary-about-what-we-just-read territory. Real navel-gazing territory.

Aww, you flatter me. I'm working on modifying and formatting the files now. What do you need them for?

Have to go do a presentation for some site directors.

Ding! They're finished. I've taken the liberty of highlighting changes from a previous iteration in green.

Indispensable is too weak a word for you, Enkidu.
• SCP-4410, Selected Iteration 1, 02/02/2008
• SCP-4410, Selected Iteration 2, 07/20/2018
• SCP-4410, Selected Iteration 3, 11/03/2021
• Partial Transcript of Emergency Meeting, 01/03/2022
• Addendum added to the SCP-4410 file, 02/10/2022

Item #: SCP-4410

Object Class: Euclid

Special Containment Procedures: By an 8-5 vote of the O5 Council, SCP-4410 will be allowed to remain on the internet, accessible to the public. The writings on the SCP-4410 website are to be monitored by Foundation personnel and Foundation-made pattern-recognition algorithms for similarities to existing anomalies.

This could all be simplified – accessible to the public is a no-brainer, and you could just say it’s checked by a webcrawler – specifying that humans read it too is a rather less-special, more generic procedure and doesn’t add anything in terms of interest and narrative. (See my comment about unnecessary narrative complexity further down.)

All SCP-4410-1 instances are to be identified and monitored. Proposals to further interrogate or test SCP-4410-1 instances must be approved by the SCP-4410 research lead. SCP-4410-1 instances should be amnesticized to remove memories of interactions with Foundation personnel, but personnel should not attempt to remove memories of SCP-4410 itself.

Shorten, simplify language. There’s too much mundane/"standard" procedure here and sentences that can be shortened and combined, e.g. “SCP-4410 instances are to be monitored and may be interrogated or tested with approval of the Lead Researcher.” The amnestic line serves a purpose, so it’s good.

Description: SCP-4410 is a fictional organization known as the PAC Institute, a global, super-national entity dedicated to the containment of anomalies and the preservation of normalcy.
If an individual who is introduced to the concept of SCP-4410 subsequently feels inclined to create stories about or related to it, they (henceforth designated SCP-4410-1) will be subject to SCP-4410's anomalous effects.

The majority of SCP-4410 stories created by SCP-4410-1 instances bear striking similarities to actual anomalies and events documented by the SCP Foundation. Furthermore, SCP-4410 itself bears striking similarities to the SCP Foundation, in terms of its goals, methods, and organizational structure.

This second sentence is way too long. You tell this stuff in much more detail and in a more interesting form below, so you don’t need this list.

Though in principle these similarities could be due to non-anomalous coincidence, the chances of such similarities occurring incidentally are so low that the Foundation considers this possibility negligible.

Overly complex grammar/detail, could be shortened.

Notable Features of SCP-4410:
• The PAC Institute gives anomalies it contains designations consisting of PAC followed by three numerals.
• Like the SCP Foundation, PAC is a double entendre which refers both to the motto of the organization (Preserve, Acquire, Contain) and the methods by which it contains anomalies (Protocols for the Anomaly's Containment).
• The PAC Institute classifies anomalies with the object classes Pandora, Yesod, and Fenris, which are identical in meaning and use to the Safe, Euclid, and Keter classes respectively.
• The PAC Institute has a clearance level system identical to the Foundation's.
• The PAC Institute employs Special Response Teams (SRTs), which are analogous to Mobile Task Forces.
• The PAC Institute employs Class-C Personnel, which are analogous to the Foundation's D-Class Personnel.
• PAC Institute operations are centered around Bases, the largest and most significant of which is Base-27, which are headed by Base Directors. Base Directors answer to the A5-Council, which has 13 members, each referred to with a numerical designation.
Investigation into currently known SCP-4410-1 instances indicate to a high degree of certainty that none of them are aware of the existence of the anomalous in general or the Foundation in particular.

”To a high degree of certainty” is a needless qualifier, what a stats professor I once had called "weasel words." It sounds academic-ish but adds nothing in context that readers could care about. Absolutely don’t tell me what we don’t know,that's the most basic version of a waste of space. “the existence of” is redundant.

To an SCP-4410-1 instance, conceiving of an SCP-4410 related story is no different than creating any other story. The mechanism by which SCP-4410 story concepts are transferred into the minds of SCP-4410-1 instances is unknown. Memetic analysis has revealed no memetic agents that could bestow information in any SCP-4410 work or the concept of SCP-4410 in general. Testing of D-Class SCP-4410-1 instances under controlled conditions have indicated that no psionic transfer of information is occurring either. Indeed, it is possible that no information transfer is responsible for SCP-4410's effects, and instead the effects are caused by anomalously induced coincidence.

Every single word beginning with “Memetic analysis” is a complete waste of space. You’ve already told us twice that we don’t know how this info is transferred: Once right before the list and once again at the start of this paragraph. I don’t care how we learned that we don’t know a thing. A lack of information is not an exciting or enticing use of my time.

The first known piece of SCP-4410 media was posted on the /x/ (Paranormal) board of the imageboard 4chan on June 7th, 2007. It took the form of the primary documentation of PAC-244, a statue that becomes highly mobile and hostile when direct line of sight is not maintained, which clearly parallels SCP-173. Several other users, inspired by the work, became SCP-4410-1 instances and created their own PAC documents, most of which paralleled existing SCP objects such as SCP-076, SCP-087, and SCP-682.

”inspired by the work” is pretty obvious. Simplify. You could footnote the list.

PAC Institute threads became very popular on the board, leading to irritation from users who felt they were crowding out other discussion. As a result, SCP-4410-1 instances transferred their work to EditThis, a public wiki-farm. Foundation webcrawlers discovered this board on July 5th, 20071. By this time, numerous stories had been created, among which were parallels of SCP-008, SCP-096, SCP-239, SCP-343, SCP-529, SCP-882, and SCP-914, among others.

Among others was already implied. Footnoting the list would be highly recommended here, I don’t need to scan over this long list in real time.

Most notably, one work seemed to parallel an as of yet undesignated anomaly contained by the Foundation2, but was written four days before the Foundation learned of the anomaly's existence. Since its initial discovery by the Foundation, SCP-4410 documents have led to the discovery of 2 anomalous objects.

”Most” before notably is a waste of space. "By the Foundation" is implied and a waste of space. We should also talk about unnecessarily complicating the narrative rather than overcomplicating the language. The point and new info of this paragraph is that 4410 has helped us acquire new things. What good does it do the reader to know one of them was undesignated and in custody BUT that is was contained after it was written about. At best, this detail adds “mundane realism”, and that’s a terrible reason for including something and wasting my time.

It has been proposed that the Foundation should attempt to support and popularize the SCP-4410 website and community, and raise SCP-4410's profile in the public consciousness so that more useful intelligence can be gleaned from related stories and so that, in the event of an LV-Class Lifted Veil Scenario3, the widespread shock of learning of the existence of the Foundation will be reduced due to familiarity with a similar, fictional organization. The merits of this proposal are currently under debate.

Almost this entire paragraph is one sentence. Woof. Simplify. “can be gleaned from related stories” is very obvious given the previous paragraph. “will be reduced due to familiarity with a similar, fictional organization” feels like an overly busy statement on this thought.

Addendum - Commentary on SCP-4410 Fiction:

Though superficially similar to SCP documentation (Consisting of containment procedures and a description), SCP-4410 writings would be considered very unprofessional by today's standards. They are often un-clinical, often use imperial units, use ALL CAPS for emphasis, and often include unimportant details and phrases regarding researcher's opinions of or attitudes towards the object or its protocols for containment. That said, the style on display in SCP-4410 works is reminiscent in some ways of documentation I've read from the early 1900s era of the Foundation.

Here we’re sailing into the becalmed waters of pure exposition, proceed with caution! Exposition is an easy place to lose reader interest in the best cases, don't amplify that problem. “(Consisting of containment procedures and a description)”, has already been covered, don’t waste my time. “the style on display in SCP-4410 works “ could just be “this style”, since the rest is obvious. “I’ve read” may be commenting/superfluous. You could just word the last bit with a lot fewer clauses, e.g., "is reminiscent in some ways of early 1900's Foundation documentation."

There is an interesting attitude of collaboration among SCP-4410-1 instances. Often they'll post a new PAC file, go to bed, and the next day see that another user has modified their work in some way, adding a new idea. This practice of editing the work of others is welcomed, so long as the modifications improve the original work. Interestingly, when this happens to articles that have noted similarities to actual anomalies in containment, the revisions that are well received often make the PAC file mirror the corresponding SCP file even more closely than before.

” and the next day see that another user has modified their work in some way, adding a new ide “ feels over-labored. “have noted similarities to actual objects in containment” is over-complex. “even more closely than before” is getting into wasted adverb territory, simplify.

I now want to shift focus, and talk about the PAC Files by themselves, on their own terms, as works of fiction.

Commenting about what you are saying in a section that is pure exposition is an absolute waste of space. Remember what I said about self-commentary \rehashing what we’ve just read or are about to read.) We’re already in exposition (failure-to-show-don’t-tell territory), just move on to the talking.

Being made in an open community of amateur writers, they are not all winners. They are often simple, even crude works of fiction. But I do not wish to disparage them, because in many of them there is an elegance in their simplicity. They are straightforward, easily digestable accounts of disturbing monsters and cursed artifacts. These stories haven't gained such popularity for no reason, after all. A perfect example of this is the original, PAC-244. The PAC-244 file is anything but conventional, it doesn't rely on a surprise or repulsive imagery for horror, as much other horror fiction on /x/ does. The brilliance of PAC-244 is that it inspires horror and wonder in the same breath. It isn't a ghost, or vampire, or zombie, or any other conventional monster that readers have a strong background understanding of. It's novel, and this inspires both fear and curiosity. Even better is that big serial number: 244, implying there are at least 243 other things as strange and mysterious as this statue. It doesn't just scare, it fires the imagination as well.

I don’t have a specific breakdown here, but it is definitely into gilding the lily, with extra clauses and examples crammed into sentences that are already very tightly packed with info. “But I do not wish to disparage them” doesn’t flow realistically and is over-complex.

This resonates with me personally, it takes me back to when I was a fresh-faced post doc, got recruited by an international conspiracy and was shown the new frontier of human knowledge that was under my nose my entire life. I spent hours on end on my new work PC reading the files that all the new recruits are shown (One of which was SCP-173). I was horrified, and entranced.

This is good as it gets at the personal angle, but there’s definitely overcomplexity that feels fake and drags this punchy section down. “that was under my nose my entire life” is at least one too many modifiers. “One of which was SCP-173” could just be “including SCP-173.

Online collaborations such as this are often fleeting, but there are some very passionate SCP-4410-1 instances. I'm hoping this community can stay active for three, maybe four years. We'll see.
— Latisha Benson, Foundation Department of Literature.

I’m stopping there arbitrarily, but I can definitely tell you that the final presentation description that Zach shares with Enkidu is definitely influenced by these pitfalls (needlessly complex grammar, purple prose/unrealistic/overly-formal dialogue for its own sake, wasting time commenting on things it just said while talking (especially during exposition, which is already an opportunity for the reader to lose interest.) The function of this section (e.g., the new information and the emotional hit you are hoping to deliver) is that the escapist events may just be predicting our future. How much text that we “are already familiar with” (as you directly say) do you need to tell us when we literally just read it. (I don’t mind fun asides in dialogue, that’s a tradition with orientations, but it can be overdone and mixes in an odd way with the overly-formal language.) When do you get to this new idea and wham line, and how much of this paragraph is actually building up to the emotion of that line?

So after that sleep-deprived ramble, some key take-aways:

+ show block
– hide block

- Every word should serve a purpose - Advance the narrative, provide necessary info, or at the very least establish tone or verisimilitude. Mundane realism is a bad reason for including content. Sometimes you must kill your darlings.
- Don't waste a reader's time by repeating content.
- Definitely don't waste time by telling us something we don't know and how we don't know it. (As some game designers have said, failing an investigation roll is never interesting.)
- Avoid self-commenting dialogue, which is another form of repetition.
- Simplify. Don't use more words (or narrative details) than are necessary. This includes words that are grammatically redundant (e.g., are already implied by the verb or previous statements), extraneous qualifiers (including scientific ones), extra clauses, etc.
- Be careful not to make exposition less interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The pataphysics idea was good for like one or two articles. It's way, way past it's prime. This adds nothing new to the idea and is almost pure recycling of an overused concept. It could be improved if it added a new twist that significantly put it apart from the same story we've heard dozens of times, but the basic concept just isn't novel enough to stand on it's own after so many repetitions and isn't automatically great because it's the new fad that everybody is doing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After reading this, it almost seems like it could bring about a fate similar to that of the -D-articles. The Meta aspect is the only thing going for the skip, and that is not an original concept in itself either. It relies heavily on other articles, and poorly employs numerous tropes and cliches. Honestly though, it'd be pretty funny if this did lead to a shift in attitude towards meta, since that could actually provide some substance, making the article actually have some greater effect in-universe

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow! And here I thought I was kind of talking crazy.

Well good, I'm glad. I've been thinking about writing a bit of Pataphysics, and this is definitely the direction I think it should go in…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"By jove, they've done it! This person understands exactly what I was going for!"

You nailed it. It does my heart good to know that what I wrote is able, at least in some cases, to communicate the way I think about pataphysics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, I love seeing new comments on my old skips, it means someone had an especially strong response, which I'm always interested in seeing.

I'm really glad you liked the ending. I personally like the interplay between "escapist events may be the normal next step" and how bizarre this "normal phenomenon" is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi, I'm gonna throw my hat in on this here a bit.

+ bear witness to my pathological ranting
- TAKE IT AWAY!!! TAKE IT AWAY!!!

To me, everything Cyantreuse is talking about here is exactly the reason why I.H.P.'s Proposal exists. Yes, most of the "sci-fi" material in the SCP Foundation is based on concepts that make mechanical sense, but are technologically impossible. Humes, like you said, are a great example of this.

Pataphysics (at first glance) does not follow this rule. It isn't based on mechanical logic, and isn't something that could be technologically possible in the future.

Or is it?

This is exactly the argument that IHP's Proposal makes (and, ironically, we see a similar argument at the end of this article, too). IHP asserts that the Swann line "god is a bunch of horror writers" isn't the end of the story and never was. There is far more to investigate about this subject, and in IHP's Proposal we see a bunch of researchers only beginning to scratch the surface. In 4410 over here, Saxon suggests that some of this stuff might not be anomalous at all, but is instead a natural evolution of the writing process.

In other words, this is uncharted territory. Things like Humes, while foreign, were couched in familiar-enough scientific concepts for Foundation researchers to apply the scientific method. But no one, in our reality or theirs, has stopped to consider the possibility that storytelling might be a measurable universal constant.

What if Pataphysics is a form of science? What if writing, like space and time, is a documentable natural phenomenon, and we're just waiting for the Einstein equivalent of fiction writers to come along and paint us the whole picture? It's just my interpretation, but to me that's exactly what articles like this one and IHP are suggesting.

Basically, my point is, I don't think Pataphysics says "this is the only option for existence." I think what it actually says is "this is an option for existence that we really ought to do some research on, because there's a distinct possibility that calling writing an 'art' is a misnomer."

As a side note, all the Pataphysics articles I've read have done (in my opinion) a good job of staying vague in the right places. Most of them reference multiple reality constructs that contain the Foundation, and imply that the meta stuff only applies to some of them (or maybe only one of them). Most Pataphysics articles give you a way in-article to disregard it as canon, or otherwise treat it as a canon for a different reality construct.

On a related note, from what I've seen, Multi-U and Pataphysics support each other more than anything else. Both of them use the multiverse concept to full effect. Actually, no sucking up here I swear, one of my favorite meta articles is one of yours, for basically this exact reason.

I've definitely gone on long enough, so final thoughts here. To me, one of the core tenants of the SCP Foundation is the mantra that "anything can happen." Pataphysics is a necessary, even obligatory part of that. "Anything can happen… yes, even that." So, to me, as long as Pataphysics continues to respect the Multi-U approach and acknowledge that there's reality constructs where there is no metafiction, then there's no harm in keeping it up.

Anyway, thanks for taking the time to read. No harm intended, just sharing my thoughts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this to pieces. I actually laughed out loud at it at a couple points.

I mean, I admit I'm biased, I'm a huge fan of meta. But your approach here is very unique; by the end of the article you downplay EVERYTHING and suggest that the entire phenomena is just normal development. It's like an anti-SCP in that sense. I agree with some of the reviewers here that it crosses a couple lines, but you're obviously self-aware of that, and address it within the article. So no real complaints here!

(And yeah I know this has been out for a while but I felt obliged to leave a comment; I liked it that much.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded. Every pataphysics article or tale I read seems like an interesting concept at first, but then I get sick of it constantly nudging me with its elbow and going "Eh? Ehh? See what I'm doing here?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well I look forward to more from you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really appreciate your detailed feedback. I just wrote a major content update that used several of your suggestions!.

I'm glad to see that the Foundation is eventually attempting to censor the PAC site and remove it from the internet. I just disapprove of it taking them 13 years and the identification Escapist events to realize this is a good move. While I initially thought "the widespread shock of learning of the existence of the Foundation will be reduced due to familiarity" approach was really novel and enjoyable, it doesn't hold water given the concerns that result from allowing, and even popularizing, something that so closely resembles the inter workings of the Foundation, highly classified data included. They would be really doing nothing but encouraging the dreaded LV-Class Lifted Veil Scenario they wish to avoid.

You make a strong point. I added a line suggesting the Foundation's tolerance of the PAC site was part to the plot armor granted by the Narrative entity.

In conclusion, while I think this is a quality work of pataphysics and really enjoyed your creativity, I believe the nature of the approach and the unavoidably tangled narrativic layers of self-reference inherent in this subject limit how well-received this article can be. And I hope I am wrong about that.

I really appreciate your praise. I agree with your conclusion as well, I knew from the beginning that a lot of people would really not like this, and I honestly don't feel like I'm quite good enough a writer to do the concept justice. But hey, it was successful. It's my highest rated article on the site. So I'm content.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the catch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The history/timeline table says SCP-3000 where it should say 4000. I assume that's a typo, but with meta I'm never entirely sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll straight up admit I'm not super into the pataphysics trend that has swept through some corners on the wiki, however, I'd like to think that an article that was executed well enough could still wring a +1 out of me. This is not that article I'm afraid.

Not only do you basically serve us the entire wiki as a kind of bizarro version of itself in this SCP, the handling of the authorship in the O5 discussion at the end just kills the piece for me, as it's not only rather hamhanded but also dull exposition on how this brand of pataphysics works.

Even if the above were not the case, in the end, the whole affair just rings far to close to Swann's Proposal for my liking.

Perhaps if this was more subtle about how it does business things would be different, but then again would that not just be Swann's proposal again?

-1.

item.title: Pataphysics has been around for more than a year.
item.content:encoded:
Pataphysics has been around for at least a year and a half.

SCP-3500 revision 0, made on 3 May 2017, mentions Pataphysics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m also tired of Pataphysics and to me meta articles have to be amazing to get an upvote. For instance, Ihp's proposal is meta and while it falls into the same rabbit hole all pataphysics articles do ("we can live normally despite being actually controlled by our authors and knowing we are controlled") it's charming and a beautiful read. This wasn't. This was a long exposure on meta and not much else. I liked the framework of the article, with Enkidu and the more informal tone of the researcher, but this still feels too much like a circlejerk of this wiki.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only thing this article has going for it is that it's meta, which is a recurring problem with pataphysics articles.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But I will say that describing pataphysics as a "wave" feels a bit hyperbolic to me.

All I know is that I've been on this site five 4.5?1 years, I'm active as hell, and it came out of nowhere a few weeks ago. Maybe I was missing something, or 'active' on the forums and not paying attention to all the recent articles outside of 4000.

But here's an elaboration of why I have issues with these canonical implications. This is dealing with some stuff that's damn hard to put into English but it comes with the topics. I'll try. I am open to people disagreeing with this of course, but I'm hoping that I might be able to summarize some of the conceptual issues that I and maybe other anti-pataphysics people have.

+ show block
– hide block

IMO Humes and SRAs make as much scientific sense as radiation detection does. The sci-fi part is the fact that the technology is currently impossible IRL, but the mechanism makes sense. Create specialized equipment to detect something —> determine what the equipment's response to various stimuli is —> develop a unit based upon the response and quantify your observations accordingly.

Pataphysics doesn't hold up in the same way.

Concept: "I, a Foundation employee, mirror the actions of a writer in a reality construct where I am a work of fiction."

A Multi-U look at this would have them say "I, a Foundation employee, exist here in this reality construct. There exists a reality construct where I am fictional. However, we cannot prove that the author created me, as I have always experienced my life as real, it is real according to my Kant counter, and it is real according to the normality standards my job requires me to enforce."

A pataphysics approach would be "I, a Foundation employee, exist here in this reality construct, which is actually not real and was written by someone in a different reality construct. They control my actions but somehow I'm still able to have autonomy2 and the best option is to base my actions around the implication that I'm not real."

The Multi-U approach acknowledges that reality depends upon frame of reference. There could be a reality construct in which a bunch of Eldritch gods are sitting around in their underwear playing chess, and the Foundation finds out that each of their pieces represents a famous Foundation action in history (or some shit; I'm spitballing for the sake of example). They would not all of a sudden define themselves as "welp, I guess we're actually just a game of chess!" Why? Because there could be another all-explaining situation that reveals itself. There are infinite possibilities and infinite universes and there's no reason for the Foundation to treat one as truth just because it sounds believable. How is it any more believable than other trickery brought upon by the existence of infinite realities?

Pataphysics is acknowledging our (IRL) reality construct as more real than the other ones and defining our actions on the wiki as the only real nature of existence for these characters. This is the opposite of the narrative value of having multiple reality constructs, to me.

If we take pataphysics to heart and believe what it says: Now we know what's objectively real. That's it. It's over. Pataphysics says "this is the only option for existence." Edit: Metafiction insinuates this too, but I tolerate the conclusion because characters talking reached it, and characters talking are susceptible to panicking and concluding that. The team composing this scientific document and researching this phenomenon, however, should know better than to show the same behavior.

Obligatory extra "in my opinion" because I know I just dumped a lot of shit here.

Footnotes
1. IDK, this is my second account
2. This logic or lack thereof is one of the most glaring conceptual deficiencies of pataphysics IMO

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad Cyantreuse wrote this criticism and worded it so well. I'm of the opinion that as the pataphysics movement develops, we become closer and closer to a circle-jerk about our own authorship. I appreciate the difficulty in doing meta well, and that when it is done well it is amazing, but it gets purely self-masturbatory real quick.

But damn do I love that CSS theme.

Edit: Will provide something useful when I am finished reading.

Here it is.
– hide block

Let me start with this: I stalked people’s sandboxes prior to the opening of the contest in excitement, and yours was one of them. I remember reading what was there of the draft (a paragraph or two into the description) and thinking “wow this is genuinely clever meta”. I’ve only so far thought that of Ihp’s Proposal and SCP-3309. Several times during the course of the posting period, I asked “where is that one entry and why isn’t it posted?” So I am real glad you posted this and was really happy when I identified this as that draft I had previewed.

My above comments on the Pataphysics Department do not completely apply to this article because, despite being self-centered, this one is also highly educational. You have put a lot of work into cataloging the actual history of this site and I for one really appreciate that. There may be no better place to grasp a quick synopsis of an insider’s take on the site, and for that (and because it really fits in well with the 4K contest theme) I want this to have an upvote and claim a healthy spot in Series V. The meta here even includes the pataphysics department itself, which aside from being very impressive, is done tactfully (Selected Iteration 3).

However, I teetered between an upvote and a novote throughout the read. I’m going to list these pivot points below. This is not something I usually do for published articles, but I'm doing it here because I want to see this article at its best.

The color choices in text clash with the beauty of the Pataphysics CSS imo. There have to be some more aesthetic choices there. If I had to make a recommendation, it'd be #BDB76B instead of orange. For that matter, I'm not sure another color (green) is necessary; Enkidu could simply use her own text color to highlight changes in the iterations for the reader.

A marked increase in campiness accompanies this shift.

I wasn't around for this in the site's history, so I may be missing something, but "campiness" is an incredibly poor word choice imo, especially considering the pejorative connotation. Something like "impropriety", "immodesty", or "inelegance" would do fine.

I'm glad to see that the Foundation is eventually attempting to censor the PAC site and remove it from the internet. I just disapprove of it taking them 13 years and the identification Escapist events to realize this is a good move. While I initially thought "the widespread shock of learning of the existence of the Foundation will be reduced due to familiarity" approach was really novel and enjoyable, it doesn't hold water given the concerns that result from allowing, and even popularizing, something that so closely resembles the inter workings of the Foundation, highly classified data included. They would be really doing nothing but encouraging the dreaded LV-Class Lifted Veil Scenario they wish to avoid.

Foundation agents in major search engines

IN the search engines? The grammar of this is horror-inspiring…that's enough for a new SCP premise right there. In seriousness though, this needs to be reworded a bit. "employed by" would be my suggestion.

Penknife.aic, an artificial intelligence construct calibrated to have no interest in fiction and no ability to suspend disbelief for the sake of consuming fiction,

This description may do better as a footnote.

47 Mobile Task Forces currently

While I appreciate the priority the Foundation is placing on this SCP, this is a gratuitous use of resources that stretches and tears my suspension of disbelief.

While the introduction of Escapist events adds another layer of delightful meta-play, it is a bit too convoluted and lengthy imo. Simplifying both the concept and its presentation would do the article good I believe.

The winner parallels [NARRATIVIC HAZARD REDACTED].

Very clever. (Are you going to change this now that the contest is decided?)

The moment in Iteration 3 where the PAC equivalent of this very article is referenced and in Iteration 4 when the PAC becomes the SCP Foundation, it becomes too convoluted and masturbatory imo. The dialogue leading up to the "naming" of the SCP Foundation is very unnatural and forced; probably inescapably so due to the convoluted nature of the layers.

In conclusion, while I think this is a quality work of pataphysics and really enjoyed your creativity, I believe the nature of the approach and the unavoidably tangled narrativic layers of self-reference inherent in this subject limit how well-received this article can be. And I hope I am wrong about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate your forthrightness. And I certainly can't criticize you for disliking my work on a conceptual level, that's totally fair.

If you're tired of pataphysics, there's certainly nothing wrong with that. But I will say that describing pataphysics as a "wave" feels a bit hyperbolic to me. Like, I'm thinking about it, and it seems like there are maybe 10 SCPs that deal with the Foundation engaging with it's own fictionality? And a few tales and author pages? That's not that many.

Give it a few months; pataphysics is too much of a gimmick to produce unique content after a certain period, I guarantee it.

You may well be right. However, I will say this: When I first discovered the site during the Series II era, I didn't just read SCPs, I often read the discussion pages as well. And there were a lot of people who didn't like Hume fields and Scranton Reality Anchors for, in my view at least, similar reasons to people who don't like pataphysics. They thought it was silly, a gimmick, invalidated conflict, overused, etc. I think pataphysics might surprise you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really appreciate you calling my work well executed, cause dang I was worried about that.

I think there's a ton of potential in the concept of a super-science organization that can break apart the building blocks of their fictional existence and examine the feelings and tropes their world is made up of in the same way we examine atoms and molecules.

I do want to comment on this, because this actually was something I wanted to touch on in my work, and is a big part of how I think about the department of Pataphysics. Here are some examples:

So, the O5s did the Foundation-ly thing: they thought "What are the practical implications of our fictionality?" "How can this be used to our ends?" And so, the Department of Pataphysics was born.

and

Our understanding of their tastes, standards, culture, demographics, and organization has dramatically improved, giving us a much better idea of narrative patterns to search for to locate new anomalies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see where you're coming from with the meta fatigue; I ended up no-voting this myself because, while it's well executed, clearly worked hard on, and a basic idea that I like, I feel like I've seen this before. "The act of writing SCPs in real life affects the in-universe characters" is a premise that I can agree has been done enough times that, even when done excellently as it is here, I'm not very interested in seeing again.

I disagree that pataphysics as a device is a dead end, though - I think it just hasn't reached its full potential yet because the tendency is still to focus it outward, on the interaction between the in-universe Foundation and its real life writers, rather than on the in-universe characters exploring what it means to be fictional. I think there's a ton of potential in the concept of a super-science organization that can break apart the building blocks of their fictional existence and examine the feelings and tropes their world is made up of in the same way we examine atoms and molecules. My own contest entry was an (admittedly clumsy and poorly thought out) attempt to use pataphysics that way; I think as time goes on, we as a site will figure out how to use this concept better, since SCP is a uniquely qualified setting to explore it, and that it could eventually open up a lot of design space for new stories.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a very lenient canon-enforcer. I feel weird when I downvote for concept. I feel extra weird when I act like a pedant about a particular canonical element someone introduces and actually bother to comment about it, but I am fundamentally opposed to this.

I am so tired of pataphysics. This is the article that's making me admit that. I don't even have the words for it. It's a wave of exhausting over-meta that is going to tire the site of meta content in general very quickly because it takes it so damn hard and makes it so overt. We could have stuck with the existence of the Multi-U Department, the implications of Swann's Proposal, and Timeline R-zayin-H-517/6. That was enough. I respect any author's approach to meta, as its very subjective, but this pataphysics business is almost objectively going to burn up all the site's meta juice. Give it a few months; pataphysics is too much of a gimmick to produce unique content after a certain period, I guarantee it.

There ain't shit I can do about it other than be annoyed, and the upvotes prove that I'm in the minority, but it's not something I can let myself be quiet about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh.

See, my knee-jerk reaction is to chastise this for being unduly self-congratulatory, but I realize all too well how hypocritical that is because, y'know, I write SCPs too. :B

The only real criticism — other than the length, I suppose — is that it's too much like Swann's Proposal, writ large, to the point of referencing it in-text. And… that's not really enough to prevent me from upvoting after the contest, at least. This is pataphysics done not excessively aggravating, which I know is faint praise.

Meanwhile, fix your "it's"es. Just about every single one should be "its". (The rule of thumb: if you can replace it with "it is", then use "it's".)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, I'm really glad it was effective.

Could you elaborate on what, specifically, you're referring to as redundant portions?

Your point about the color is fair, I'll look at some options.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's So Meta Even This Acronym.

You talked to me about this at the start of the contest, and I whole-heartedly approved of the idea. I wish I'd gotten to read this before now, but even as a coldpost, this is great. It broke my brain trying to read this, but in a good way.

One thing I'd fix up after voting period ends: you don't need a lot of redundant portions. There's an article which excises redundant portions in the revisions.

Also: mind changing the alternate text color from Green to something else? Doesn't stand out too well against the Pataphysics theme.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure how much I like this. It's deliberatly very meta, and I feel that the fourth iteration takes that too far.

On the other hand, it's a fun read, and feels like an excellent homage to the history of the site.

+1

item.title: AUTHOR POST
item.content:encoded:
HOLY MOLY I PROCRASTINATED AND JUST GOT THIS IN ON TIME. I HAD TO COLDPOST.

There will be more content to come.

Well, an Edit to this comment is a long time coming.
First off, here's my author page if you're interested in my other work: http://www.scp-wiki.net/author-page-zzuxon

At the risk of sounding like I'm tooting my own horn, I adore how 4410 turned out. Everything was riding against it. It's a pataphysics SCP, which many on the site have an automatic distaste for. It's an entry to the 4000 contest, so standards are automatically higher than your everyday skip. It is very ambitious, and therefore very easy to screw up. The basic premise is "The SCP wiki is an anomaly documented by the Foundation," which has also been the premise of many a new user coldpost that got soundly elbow dropped into deletion. Most of all, it was a coldpost itself.

In spite of all that, it is now my highest rated SCP by a wide margin. +67 may not seem like much, and it isn't, really. But I honestly doubted I would ever hit +50, and now I have, on the work I feel is the best and most worthy to have my highest rating.

The idea for 4410 originated in seeing a joke on /r/DankMemesfromSite19 which suggested that by Series XI, cognitohazards would crawl out of your computer screen and punch you in the face. The joke of course was about the way that SCPs and site expectations have tended to become more complex over time. I imagined site users eventually figuring out how to make actual anomalies to satisfy the communities hunger for increasingly sophisticated and immersive fiction. I originally conceptualized it as a joke 001 proposal, then realized it could be portrayed seriously as well, and thought that it would be my serious 001 proposal one day. One day being far off, because I was very intimidated by how high-concept the idea was. I thought I'd screw it up if I didn't have enough SCPs under my belt. But when I learned the 4000 contest theme was history, I knew I had to write it before I felt ready. I didn't feel like I was a good enough writer to do the concept justice, and I kind of still don't. But I do think it works, it does what I wanted it to do. I'm proud.

I want to give big thanks to Roget and /u/modulum83 (Probably a user here too, someone please tell me who he is on wikidot), whose History of the Universe and Modern Introduction to the SCP Foundation: Part 2 respectively were invaluable resources and influences for this skip. In particular, a portion of Dr. Benson's second note was based so heavily on a portion of Modern Introduction to the SCP Foundation: Part 2 that I want to attribute it as a source.

Also big thanks to FloppyPhoenix for the slick AF Pataphysics CSS.
